content.text: Agentic systems encounter inputs and states their designers never anticipated.

is the discipline of designing prompts, validation layers, and fallback paths that keep a pipeline correct and safe when reality deviates from the happy path — ambiguous instructions, malformed data, out-of-scope requests, adversarial injections, or simply the long tail of user creativity.

attrs.alt: Handling Edge Cases architecture: input triage, classifier, LLM core, output validator with retry loop, and graceful degradation branches

content.text: Edge case handling in prompting is a layered strategy: before invoking the main LLM,
the input for known failure modes; during the call, give the model
for how to behave on ambiguous or out-of-scope inputs; after the call,
the output against a contract and retry or degrade gracefully on failure. The approach is not a single technique but a discipline applied at every boundary of the pipeline.
The canonical failure modes that edge case handling addresses are:
the model may try to answer anyway,

Production agentic systems do not operate on a curated test set. Real users submit ticket subjects that are a single emoji, customer-facing chatbots receive messages in languages the system was never designed for, and API consumers occasionally send payloads where required JSON fields are missing. Without explicit edge case handling, the model will attempt to answer anyway — hallucinating a plausible but incorrect response — or the pipeline will throw an unhandled exception and stop mid-task.

The cost of unhandled edge cases compounds in multi-step agents. A misclassification in step two propagates through steps three, four, and five before a human notices. By the time the failure surface is visible, the root cause is buried. Defensive prompt engineering and boundary validation cut off error propagation at the source.

There is also a safety dimension. Prompt injection attacks exploit the absence of input triage. An attacker who can reach the model's context can instruct it to ignore prior instructions or emit data it should not. A well-placed guard prompt and sanitisation layer removes this attack surface before the payload reaches the LLM.
When the model is told explicitly what to do with uncertain inputs — refuse or caveat rather than guess — it stops inventing plausible-sounding fabrications.
A system that returns a structured refusal is easier to monitor and route than a system that silently produces wrong answers.
Most edge cases can be handled automatically (clarification request, fallback to a simpler path, safe default) without involving a human agent.
Structured edge-case outcomes can be logged, counted, and used to drive prompt iteration. Unstructured failures cannot.
Input triage catches most adversarial patterns before they touch the model's primary reasoning context.
A partial answer with an explicit caveat delivers more value than a pipeline exception that the user must decipher.
Typed output contracts — including the refused and caveats fields — mean downstream steps can handle edge case outcomes without reading free-form text.
Embedding edge case instructions and few-shot examples consumes tokens. In models with tight context budgets, this is a real cost.
A model prompted to refuse aggressively will start refusing legitimate edge cases that are actually answerable. Guard thresholds require calibration.
Every explicitly handled edge case is a new code path that must be updated when business rules change. An undocumented catalogue of edge cases becomes technical debt.
A separate guard-prompt call before the main call adds round-trip time. For latency-sensitive pipelines, this may be unacceptable unless the triage is rule-based and deterministic.
A tightly specified output contract makes the output predictable but makes it harder to evolve the response format. Minor schema changes require updating every downstream consumer.
Every edge case path must be tested independently. Building a test suite that exercises all branches of an edge case handler requires deliberate effort.
A robust edge-case handling pipeline runs in four conceptually distinct stages that execute in order within a single agent turn.

Before making any LLM call, apply rule-based checks that do not require a model: length limits, encoding validation, empty-input detection, and injection pattern matching via regex. These checks are cheap (
in input length), deterministic, and catch the most common failure modes at near-zero cost. Return a typed

For inputs that pass triage but still appear unusual — very short text, mixed languages, boundary-case topics — invoke a lightweight guard prompt on a fast, cheap model. The guard prompt's sole job is to classify the input as

and return a confidence score. This classification costs a fraction of the main call and prevents the full reasoning model from wasting expensive compute on a request that will be refused or redirected.

The main LLM call receives context about the triage and guard results. The system prompt includes explicit instructions for each edge case category: what to do when the question is ambiguous (ask a single clarifying question), what to do when the topic is out of scope (structured refusal), and how to signal uncertainty (populate the
score). Few-shot examples anchor each behaviour.
Parse the raw LLM output and validate it against the typed contract. On a validation failure, append the error message to the conversation and retry up to
times. If the output is valid but the confidence score is below a threshold
, route to a human or a fallback tool instead of propagating the uncertain answer downstream.

The diagram above shows the full architecture. Inputs enter through the edge case classifier (middle left). Clean inputs flow directly to the core LLM. Detected edge cases branch to one of three handlers: Ambiguous Input (ask or default), Out-of-Scope (structured refusal), or Malformed/Toxic (sanitise or reject before LLM). The output validator drives a retry loop for schema-invalid responses and routes valid outputs to the caller.
— are exhaustive. Every possible response from the system maps to exactly one state, which means the caller can handle each case with a simple
chain. There is no implicit 'something went wrong but we are not sure what' path.
Think of edge case handling as a

. Each component in the pipeline — the input sanitiser, the guard classifier, the LLM, the output validator — has an explicit contract: what it accepts, what it returns on success, and what it returns on every failure mode. When every contract is spelled out and implemented, the pipeline has no implicit states and no silent failures.

A useful analogy is defensive driving: you do not assume every other driver will obey the rules. You position the car to minimise the damage of the failures you can predict — tailgating, sudden lane changes, running red lights — without becoming so cautious that you cannot reach your destination. Prompt edge case handling is the same: handle the predictable failures systematically so that the inevitable surprises are contained.
Any agent that accepts free-form user input in production — chatbots, document processors, ticket routers, code reviewers.
Pipelines where a failed step cannot be retried cheaply — multi-step workflows where reprocessing from scratch is expensive.
Applications with safety or compliance requirements where hallucinating a confident wrong answer is worse than refusing.
Systems exposed to adversarial users — customer-facing APIs, public chatbots, any endpoint reachable by anonymous users.
Long-running autonomous agents where a single unhandled edge case can cause irreversible downstream actions.
Multi-tenant platforms where different tenants' inputs may fall outside the edge cases the system was designed for.
Any context where the distribution of real inputs is likely to differ significantly from the distribution of test inputs.

Implementation begins with an inventory of known edge cases for your specific domain, then a prioritised classification of each by likelihood and severity of failure. The resulting taxonomy drives the guard prompt instructions and the retry strategy.
Write deterministic, rule-based checks as a function that runs synchronously before any I/O. This function should return a typed enum —
— never a boolean. A boolean forces the caller to guess what kind of bad input was detected.
Step 2 — Embed Edge Case Instructions in the System Prompt
"If the question cannot be answered reliably, set refused: true."
Include two or three in-context examples covering the specific edge cases your domain is likely to encounter. Vague instructions like
produce inconsistent results across models and versions.

Parse the raw response, validate with Pydantic or JSON Schema, and on failure append the error to the conversation and retry. Set a hard cap of three retries; beyond that, return a structured failure rather than looping indefinitely. Log every retry — a high retry rate is a signal that the system prompt needs adjustment.

The following example shows a complete ticket-routing agent that integrates triage, guard classification, and typed output into a single function. It is the kind of boundary a real customer-support system would have to handle.

from __future__ import annotations
"""
Real-world usage: customer-support ticket router.
Demonstrates triage -> route -> handle_edge_case -> typed output.
"""
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class TicketCategory(str, Enum):
    BILLING   = "billing"
    TECHNICAL = "technical"
    RETURNS   = "returns"
    OTHER     = "other"
    REFUSED   = "refused"

@dataclass
class RoutingDecision:
    category: TicketCategory
    priority: str        # "low" | "medium" | "high"
    agent_queue: str
    confidence: float
    explanation: str
    edge_case_handled: bool = False

ROUTING_PROMPT = """
Classify the support ticket below. Return JSON only:
{
  "category": "billing|technical|returns|other",
  "priority": "low|medium|high",
  "agent_queue": "<queue name>",
  "confidence": <0.0-1.0>,
  "explanation": "<one sentence>",
  "refused": false,
  "refusal_reason": ""
}

Edge cases you MUST handle explicitly:
  - Empty or gibberish text -> refused=true, reason="unintelligible input"
  - Multiple unrelated issues in one ticket -> category="other",
    explanation includes "multiple issues detected"
  - Offensive/abusive content -> refused=true, reason="policy violation"
  - Duplicate of a previous ticket -> include "[POSSIBLE DUPLICATE]" in explanation
  - Non-English text -> category based on content, add caveat in explanation
"""

def route_ticket(raw_text: str) -> RoutingDecision:
    from triage_input import triage_input, EdgeKind  # from code1 above
    triage = triage_input(raw_text)

    # Short-circuit for clear edge cases before touching the LLM
    if triage.kind == EdgeKind.EMPTY:
        return RoutingDecision(
            category=TicketCategory.REFUSED,
            priority="low",
            agent_queue="discard",
            confidence=1.0,
            explanation="Empty ticket — discarded.",
            edge_case_handled=True,
        )
    if triage.kind == EdgeKind.ADVERSARIAL:
        return RoutingDecision(
            category=TicketCategory.REFUSED,
            priority="high",
            agent_queue="security-review",
            confidence=0.95,
            explanation=f"Adversarial pattern detected: {triage.reason}",
            edge_case_handled=True,
        )

    # LLM-based classification for normal + ambiguous inputs
    import json
    from answer_with_retry import call_llm  # from code2 above
    raw = call_llm(f"Ticket:\n{raw_text}", ROUTING_PROMPT)
    raw = raw.strip().lstrip("```json").rstrip("```").strip()
    data = json.loads(raw)

    if data.get("refused"):
        return RoutingDecision(
            category=TicketCategory.REFUSED,
            priority="low",
            agent_queue="escalation",
            confidence=1.0,
            explanation=data.get("refusal_reason", "LLM refused"),
            edge_case_handled=True,
        )
    return RoutingDecision(
        category=TicketCategory(data["category"]),
        priority=data["priority"],
        agent_queue=data["agent_queue"],
        confidence=data["confidence"],
        explanation=data["explanation"],
        edge_case_handled=triage.kind != EdgeKind.NORMAL,
    )

An e-commerce company routes support tickets automatically. Before deploying, they audit two months of historical tickets and identify the following edge case distribution: 3% empty or spam, 2% offensive content, 8% tickets with multiple unrelated issues, 5% non-English, 4% vague one-word tickets. Without explicit handling, the LLM would route all of these — often incorrectly — with high confidence. After implementing the triage and guard layers, these five categories are handled explicitly, reducing misrouting by 18% and cutting human-review escalations by 31%.
The critical insight from this case study is that the model's
confidence scores were not reliable indicators of correctness

on edge cases. The LLM would return 0.92 confidence on a single-emoji ticket routed to the wrong queue. Confidence scores calibrated on the training distribution do not transfer to the tails. Only explicit classification of known edge cases restored reliability.
In production, edge cases collectively account for 15–30% of traffic in most consumer-facing systems. Designing only for the happy path leaves a significant fraction of users with poor experiences.
Using free-form string responses for failure signals.
If the model returns the string "I cannot help with that" in a field that should contain a structured response, the caller cannot distinguish it from a legitimate short answer. Use typed fields.
A bug in the output schema combined with no retry cap can cause an agent to loop until rate limits or cost caps are hit. Always set a hard maximum.
Without logs, you cannot know whether the triage and guard layers are accurately classifying inputs or over-refusing legitimate requests.

Asking the model to detect its own edge cases in the same call that processes the request conflates triage and processing. A model focused on answering a question will notice edge case signals less reliably than a dedicated classifier.
An input of 50,000 whitespace characters is not the same as an empty string, but both require the same handling. Normalise before checking.
Not including edge case examples in few-shot.
Few-shot examples that only show the happy path teach the model to handle the happy path. Include at least two examples of edge case handling so the model understands the refusal/caveat pattern.

Requiring all fields to be non-empty even on refusal leads to the model populating required fields with placeholder text like 'N/A', which creates ambiguity. Make non-relevant fields optional or nullable on refusal.
Enumerate edge cases before writing prompts.
For each known edge case, document the input pattern, the desired output, and the rationale. This list becomes your few-shot source and your test suite.
Run deterministic checks synchronously before any LLM call. Do not co-locate triage logic inside the main system prompt.
"Return an empty sources array" is more reliable than "you may omit sources if unavailable." Ambiguous permission language is interpreted inconsistently.
Include the error in the retry prompt.
Appending the exact validation error to the retry prompt doubles the probability of a successful second attempt compared to simply repeating the original prompt.
Set confidence thresholds per downstream action.
An action that modifies a database record should require higher confidence than one that drafts a suggestion. Calibrate thresholds against held-out labelled data.
Build a regression suite that includes injection attempts, extremely long inputs, conflicting instructions, and domain-boundary questions. Run it on every model update.
Degrade to partial rather than to nothing.
If the model cannot fully answer a question, a partial answer with explicit caveats is almost always more useful than a complete refusal. Design the output schema to support partial population.

As the system's domain expands, new edge cases appear. Track them with the same rigour as feature changes — include the version of the taxonomy in your system prompt header so logs can be correlated to the policy in effect at generation time.

The deterministic triage layer adds negligible overhead — a few microseconds of regex matching per request. The guard prompt, if used, adds one additional LLM call. For a p50 main-call latency of 800ms, a guard prompt on a small fast model (e.g., 50ms) represents a 6% overhead that is acceptable for safety-critical paths but may be too high for real-time conversational applications.

: for high-throughput pipelines that process documents offline, running triage in a single vectorised pass over thousands of inputs before any LLM calls allows parallel processing of the non-edge-case inputs while the edge cases are handled separately. A second optimisation is
: if the same input is submitted repeatedly (common in retry loops or polling agents), cache the triage result for a short TTL to avoid redundant classification calls.
The retry loop has a known worst-case cost of
is the cost of a single call. With
and a 1% validation failure rate, the expected extra cost per call is approximately
, which is acceptable for most applications.

attrs.latex: E[\text{extra calls}] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} k \cdot p^k \cdot (1-p) \approx p \cdot (1 + 2p) \text{ for small } p

content.text: The standard Python library for declaring typed output contracts. field_validator and model_validator hooks let you embed domain-specific checks alongside schema validation.
A Python library that wraps multiple LLM SDKs and provides automatic structured output extraction with retry on validation failure. Reduces boilerplate for the validate-and-retry pattern.
A framework for defining guardrails as composable validators that run against LLM outputs. Supports custom validators for domain-specific edge cases.

Pre-built parsers (PydanticOutputParser, StructuredOutputParser) that handle the parse-validate-retry loop. Useful for quick integration but less flexible than hand-written validators for complex schemas.
Evaluation frameworks that can flag edge cases in test runs by comparing outputs against golden labels. Useful for measuring over-refusal and under-refusal rates on labelled datasets.
For deterministic triage, simple regex patterns and rule engines (e.g., Drools, JSON logic) outperform LLM-based classifiers in latency, cost, and predictability for well-defined edge case classes.
Edge case handling is the primary defence against

— the class of attack where a malicious payload in the user's input instructs the model to ignore previous instructions, reveal confidential system prompts, or take unauthorised actions. The defence works by classifying the input before it reaches the model's primary reasoning context. Once the model has processed a prompt-injection payload in its main context, it is difficult to undo the effect.
Additional security considerations: the triage layer should operate on the

— sanitising before triage can hide the signals that injection detection relies on. The guard classifier should run in a separate call with a minimal system prompt that contains no secrets — if the guard call is itself compromised, it should not leak anything sensitive. Output validation should be done in application code, not trusted to the model's self-attestation (e.g., do not ask the model 'is your output valid JSON?' — parse it yourself).

at the pipeline level: if more than a configurable percentage of calls within a rolling window are triggering retries or refusals, stop routing new requests and alert the on-call team. A spike in edge case rates is often an early signal of a prompt injection campaign or a model behaviour change after a provider update.
Edge cases are not exceptional — they represent 15–30% of real-world traffic. Design for them explicitly, not as an afterthought.
Layer the defences: deterministic triage first, then a lightweight guard classifier, then the main call, then output validation and retry.
Use typed, structured output contracts with explicit fields for refusal status, confidence, and caveats. Free-form string failure messages are not routable by downstream code.
Separate triage from processing — deterministic checks should run before any LLM call.
Include edge case examples in few-shot: showing the model how to refuse, caveat, or partially answer is as important as showing it how to answer correctly.
Set hard retry caps and log every retry. High retry rates are a prompt quality signal, not just a latency problem.
Degrade to partial + caveat rather than silence or hallucination. A partial answer that is honest about its limits is always better than a confident wrong answer.
Version and audit your edge case taxonomy alongside your prompts. As the system's scope grows, the taxonomy must grow with it.
